name: Patterns — What Comes Next?
whatIsIt: A PATTERN is something that repeats in a rule. 🔴🔵🔴🔵🔴🔵 repeats red-blue. A clap-stomp-clap-stomp is a sound pattern. Numbers make patterns too: 2, 4, 6, 8 jumps by 2. Find the RULE, and you can always tell what comes next.
whyNeeded: Patterns are how we spot order in the world — in music, in rangoli, in days of the week, in counting. Finding the rule trains your brain to predict, which is the heart of maths.
realLifeUses: Rangoli and border designs repeat a unit
Days of the week repeat in the same order
Counting by 2s, 5s and 10s makes number patterns

vocabulary.meaning: Something that repeats following a rule.
The part that repeats, or the jump each time.
Using the rule to find the following item.

story.title: Dadi's Rangoli
story.text: Dadi makes a rangoli border: flower, dot, flower, dot, flower, dot. 'You finish it, Riya,' she says. Riya looks carefully. 'The rule is flower then dot, again and again. So after this last dot comes a flower!' She adds a flower and the border marches on, neat and pretty.
story.extractedProblem: The border goes flower, dot, flower, dot, flower, dot… what comes next?
story.answerInStory: A flower — the rule is flower-then-dot repeating.

visual.caption: Rule: red, blue, repeating. After blue comes red — so the ❓ is red.

teachingGallery.title: Find the rule, say what's next
teachingGallery.note: Read the repeating unit, then continue it.

examples.caption: Rule star-moon → next is a star.
The unit is 3 long → next is a triangle.

standardMethod.summary: Find the repeating unit (or the jump), then continue the rule.
steps: Look for the part that repeats — say it out loud: 'red, blue, red, blue'.
For numbers, find the jump: how much bigger each time?
Use the rule to write or say what comes next.
The repeating unit is green, green, yellow.

commonMistakes.mistake: Guessing the next item without finding the full repeating unit.
commonMistakes.fix: Say the whole repeating part out loud (like 'red-blue') before choosing what comes next.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using the wrong jump in a number pattern (e.g. adding 1 instead of 2).
commonMistakes.fix: Check the jump between TWO pairs of numbers to be sure, then keep adding the same jump.

hintLadder: Say it: red, blue, red, blue…
Find the jump: it goes up by 2.
The unit is star, moon, moon.

challenge.q: Ravi sees 3, 6, 9, 12 and says the next is 13. What's the real rule?

options.label: Add 3 each time, so next is 15

challenge.answer: Add 3 each time, so next is 15

questions.q: The BEST first step to solve a pattern is to…

options.label: Find the repeating unit or the jump

questions.answer: Find the repeating unit or the jump

teachBackPrompt: Make your own pattern with crayons, claps, or toys (like red-red-blue repeating). Show a grown-up, ask them what comes next, and then explain the rule you used.

revisionCard.summary: A pattern repeats a RULE. Find the repeating unit (red-blue) or the jump (add 2), then continue it to say what comes next. Check the jump on two pairs to be sure.

rememberIt.hook: A pattern REPEATS - find the part that repeats
rememberIt.unpack: AB AB AB (red-blue), or ABB ABB. Say it out loud to hear the repeat, then predict what comes next.

studentQuestions.q: How do I find what comes next?
studentQuestions.a: Spot the part that repeats (the 'core'), then keep repeating it. Red-blue-red-blue -> next is red.
